model.label: Kimi K2.5
model.description: Moonshot AI • Reasoning model from the Kimi family built for complex thinking tasks.

pick.rationale: Duke holds a commanding statistical advantage as the #1 ranked team nationally with a historic +39.17 adjusted efficiency margin that exceeds St. John's +24.8 mark by nearly 15 points. The Blue Devils' top-ranked defense presents a severe mismatch against St. John's offense ranked 46th-47th nationally, while Duke's fourth-ranked offense can exploit gaps in the Red Storm's 12th-ranked defense. Although Duke remains without injured starters Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, their dominant ACC Tournament run and first-round performances demonstrated sufficient depth to overcome a quality opponent. While Rick Pitino's veteran leadership and St. John's Big East championship momentum create a credible upset path at the volatile 1/5 seed line, Duke's overwhelming two-way efficiency and 18-spot ranking advantage provide a reliable path to the Elite Eight.

reasoningStep.title: Duke vs St. John's Sweet 16 Statistical Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Duke enters as the #1 team in the nation with historic efficiency margins (+39.17) and the country's top-ranked defense. St. John's is a quality 5-seed with a strong defense (#12) but a significant offensive mismatch against Duke's elite unit (#46-47 offense vs #1 defense). Duke's missing players (Foster, Ngongba) have not hindered their dominance. The ~15-point efficiency gap and defensive superiority make Duke a clear favorite despite St. John's Big East credentials and Rick Pitino's tournament experience.
evidence: Duke: KenPom #1, Torvik #1, Adj EM +39.17, Offense #4, Defense #1, 31-2 record
St. John's: KenPom #20, Torvik #18, Adj EM +24.8, Offense #46, Defense #12, 27-6 record
Duke missing Caleb Foster (broken foot) and Patrick Ngongba II (foot) since before ACC Tournament
Duke dominated ACC Tournament and first two rounds without injured players
St. John's defeated Kansas in Round 2 as narrow underdog (prior pick confidence 0.55)
St. John's enters as Big East regular season and tournament champions with Rick Pitino coaching
Efficiency margin gap of ~14.4 points favors Duke significantly

Ishika Samant / Getty Images By Ralph D. Russo March 14, 2026 Updated March 16, 2026 Share full article 88 NEW YORK — To cut the final string of net off the rim after St. John’s won its second straight Big East tournament championship, the 73-year-old Hall of Fame coach and the 21-year-old conference player of the year climbed the ladder together. Rick Pitino had his arm around Zuby Ejiofor as the Red Storm star clipped the last strand, spun the twine over his head and accepted one last ovation from adoring fans at Madison Square Garden. Advertisement “It was just bittersweet,” Ejiofor said about getting emotional after his final game at the Garden. Probably more sweet than bitter, though. Led by Ejiofor, No. 13 St. John’s repeated as Big East tournament champions with a 72-52 win over No. 6 UConn on Saturday night, a loss that might have cost the Huskies a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament. A few hours after Florida lost to Vanderbilt in the SEC tournament semifinals to further open the door to a top seed in the NCAA Tournament, Dan Hurley’s Huskies got jumped early by the Johnnies and never fully recovered. Ejiofor was a force on both ends for top-seeded St. John’s (28-6). The Big East player of the year had 18 points, nine rebounds and seven blocks. result.snippet: Top-ranked Duke has lost starting point guard Caleb Foster to a broken right foot that will keep him out for an extended period, while starting big man Patrick Ngongba II won't play in this week's Atlantic Coast Conference Tournament while dealing with his own foot issue.

result.title: Duke's Caleb Foster undergoes foot surgery, Patrick Ngongba out ... - MSN

result.title: Duke's Foster to miss at least several weeks with fracture in foot ...
result.snippet: No. 1 Duke is the top seed in the ACC Tournament, but will be without starting point guard Caleb Foster and starting center Patrick Ngongba for the event in Charlotte.

modelTrace.content:
The search results indicate that Duke is without Caleb Foster (broken foot, surgery, out several weeks) and Patrick Ngongba II (foot issue). They haven't played in the ACC Tournament and based on "several weeks" timeline, they would still be out for the Sweet 16. However, Duke has dominated without them so far.

Let me check one more article to confirm the injury timeline and see if there's any chance they return.

modelTrace.content:
The page is truncated but confirms the injuries. Based on "several weeks" for Foster and the tournament timeline, they would likely still be out for the Sweet 16.

Now I have enough information to make a prediction. Let me summarize:

1. **Duke**: 
   - #1 ranked team in the country (KenPom and Torvik)
   - +39.17 adjusted efficiency margin (historic, elite)
   - #1 defense, #4 offense
   - 31-2 record
   - Missing Caleb Foster (starting PG, broken foot) and Patrick Ngongba II (starting C, foot) - both out since before ACC Tournament
   - Dominated ACC Tournament and first two rounds without them

2. **St. John's**:
   - #18-20 ranked team (KenPom/Torvik)
   - +24.8 adjusted efficiency margin (very good but ~14 points below Duke)
   - #12 defense, #46-47 offense
   - 27-6 record
   - Big East regular season and tournament champions
   - Rick Pitino coaching, veteran tournament experience
   - Beat Kansas in Round 2 (narrow 55% confidence upset pick in prior round)

3. **Matchup analysis**:
   - Duke's #1 defense vs St. John's #46-47 offense: This is a severe mismatch. St. John's offense will struggle.
   - Duke's #4 offense vs St. John's #12 defense: Duke can score even on a good defense.
   - ~14.4 point efficiency margin gap is huge
   - St. John's path to upset: Coaching advantage (Pitino), momentum from Big East title, and hoping Duke's missing players create vulnerability

This is a 1-seed vs 5-seed matchup. 5-seeds upset 1-seeds occasionally but it's rare (maybe 15-20% historically). Given Duke's historic efficiency margin and the fact they've already shown they can dominate without their injured players, I should pick Duke.

The confidence should be high but not 97% like the Round 1 matchup against a 16-seed. Against a quality 5-seed like St. John's, maybe around 80-85%. 

. St. John's presents a real challenge— Rick Pitino's tournament experience and their Big East credentials make this more competitive than typical early-round games. The Red Storm's defensive strength could create problems for Duke's offense, potentially keeping the game closer than anticipated. I'll go with 83% confidence for Duke advancing.

I have all the information I need. Let me now make my prediction.

Key facts:
- Duke: #1 in KenPom/Torvik, +39.17 efficiency margin, #1 defense, #4 offense, 31-2 record
- St. John's: #18-20 in KenPom/Torvik, +24.8 efficiency margin, #12 defense, #46-47 offense, 27-6 record
- Duke missing Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II but dominated without them
- St. John's beat Kansas to get here and won Big East tournament
- Rick Pitino is a veteran tournament coach

The efficiency margin gap is ~14.4 points in Duke's favor. This is significant but not insurmountable for a well-coached 5-seed. However, Duke's #1 defense is the biggest factor - St. John's offense (ranked ~47th) will struggle to score.

1-seeds vs 5-seeds historically favor the 1-seed significantly. I'll pick Duke with around 82% confidence.
